data.title: HP: Hardware Weakness Is Creating A Double-Digit Yield Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered HP Inc. (HPQ), I called them a “Severely Mispriced Cash Flow Powerhouse,” upgrading them to a Strong Buy while highlighting their strong cash flow, clear undervaluation and cost savings while adapting to the near- and medium-term macro pressure.

With the price getting more attractive despite a solid report recently, the yield and upside potential are even more impressive now, with HPQ remaining in a very solid position to take advantage of a recovery in the industry once the current headwinds are over.

Internal Developments

Although outweighed by their mixed outlook, HPQ reported a strong Q1’FY26, with a 6.9% increase in revenue, as the strong 11.1% growth in personal systems significantly offsetting the 2.2% drop in printing, while the Non-GAAP operating margin also stood at 6.9%.

HPQ remains on track to meet the $1 billion gross annualized run rate savings by the end of FY28 announced recently, although given the ongoing CEO transition, I'd wait to see an update about the new CEO's plans during their Investor Day, which was delayed from April following the sudden CEO change.

As for their FY26 outlook, HPQ continues to see about $2.8 billion to $3 billion in FCF (expected to be at the low end of the range), while expecting their revenue to grow faster than the market and moderating in the second half of the fiscal year, advancing on the cost savings I mentioned before and proving to be quite resilient despite the ongoing hardware supply-demand pressure.

Financially, based on HPQ’s latest report, we continue to see an overall solid position, with current assets below current liabilities due to how the business operates, while the $8.84 billion in debt remains manageable and well-spread over a long period, while they still have about $3.15 billion worth of cash and cash equivalents, providing some financial flexibility if needed.

As mentioned in the previous analysis, HPQ increased their dividend by 4%, currently standing at a ~6.26% dividend yield that costs about $277 million (in Q1’FY26), alongside $325 million on buybacks, which gets to an annualized ~$2.4 billion, meaning a combined yield of ~13.68%, for a payout ratio of ~82.75% based on their midpoint FCF guidance.

Given the strong cash flow and valuation, I’d strongly push for even more buybacks (even going as far as cutting the dividend for it), as this temporary weakness caused by the macro pressures I’ll mention below could be an excellent opportunity for the company to reduce their number of shares significantly.

Macro Developments

To begin with the ongoing attack against Iran, this remains a very big source of pressure and uncertainty, with oil/gas prices surging and Iran closing the Strait of Hormuz, which is key for international trade in oil, gas, fertilizers, etc., potentially leading to broader economic issues.

Although Trump said that the US Navy could escort ships, big shipping companies like Maersk (AMKBY) didn’t want to take that kind of risk, and not only did they avoid it, but also the entire region instead (including the Suez Canal), preferring to take the long Cape of Good Hope route, potentially resulting in significant delays/extra costs in global trade.

Beyond that, depending on how long this conflict will last (which I believe is entirely up to the end goal), we could even see more attacks against countries like Saudi Arabia or the UAE (where a couple of AWS (AMZN) centers were recently hit by drones), which are involved in many AI/tech/data center investments.

If the focus there suddenly shifts to increasing their military/defense capacity or they simply decide to cut back on AI spending due to the broader macro environment, we could see this as a headwind for the confidence that fueled significant gains in the stock market, although it can actually be beneficial for HPQ given the potential normalization of the hardware market.

The tariff situation can also help following the Supreme Court’s decision, although they were subsequently lifted to 15%, which HPQ’s new CEO (as the previous one went to PayPal (PYPL) not long ago, which I also rated a Strong Buy recently) said during their Q1'FY26 Earnings Call would not negatively impact them, although I would add that the situation continues to be uncertain and can always shift again:

We are evaluating the impact of the decisions including the announcement of new tariffs in the last few days. Right now, we do not expect to be negatively impacted by the subsequent developments following the court decision. However, we will continue to engage the administration on these matters and others. We are confident in the strength and agility of our supply chain, which provides the flexibility needed to navigate an uncertain trade-related cost environment.

However, don’t expect these hardware headwinds to fix overnight, as it can take months or even years for these issues to normalize, while the consumer environment continues to be under pressure, with some of the worst levels of US consumer confidence since 2014 seen recently despite some improvements and significant trade-downs even for the most basic things such as food, so delaying a laptop purchase for example at current prices doesn’t seem like such an irrational thing to do.

Still, I believe these delays are only a matter of time, as hardware will inevitably have to be replaced and HPQ should stand to benefit from the next cycle, regardless of its timing, while the company remains in a strong position from many points of view and still manages to prove their resilience. The company’s CEO also talked about this during their Q1’FY26 Earnings Call, which also highlighted their mitigation efforts, including long-term supply chain agreements (memory requirements for FY26 are covered), expanding lower-cost commodity sourcing and other supply/cost strategies:

Like others, we are seeing increased input costs driven primarily by the rising prices of DRAM and NAND. We expect this volatility to remain throughout fiscal '26 and likely into fiscal '27. While we believe the market will rationalize over time, we have already been implementing a number of mitigation measures.

This is similar to other expectations and is in line with my own as well, with IDC forecasting an 11.3% drop for the global PC market in 2026, with the market flattening in 2027 and a rebound from 2028 onwards. These couple of years of significant pressure will likely be a good time to accumulate HPQ, while the company’s cost savings and strong cash flow can be used for significant buybacks.

Risks Reiterated

Despite some improvements in terms of tariffs and rates for HPQ and its clients/partners/consumers, the macro landscape continues to be under pressure, and I don’t expect the hardware issues to be solved overnight, while the sudden CEO transition can add another layer of uncertainty that the market doesn’t appreciate.

Given the environment, it also means that it’s more difficult for HPQ to pass these extra costs to the buyer, as the ongoing macro pressure already affected the affordability of their products. Although that’s normal in a high-rate environment, this pressure is accentuated by several other macro worries.

Valuation

For HPQ’s DCF to Equity, starting with the lowest end of the FCF guidance of $2.8 billion in FY26, without accounting for net borrowing changes as I’ll consider the current level of debt to be maintained, highlighting their “pure” cash flow potential.

I’ll go for a more conservative $2.6 billion in FY27 given the potential to see them being more exposed to macro pressure (offset by cost savings), followed by a conservative return to $2.9 billion in FY28 (this year’s midpoint guidance) and a 4.5% CAGR until FY31 thanks to a modest recovery despite their significant potential to benefit from macro improvements that can support a new PC replacement cycle and buybacks helping the per share results, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for FY32 to FY36, with a 2.5% terminal growth rate and a high 12% discount rate to take into account the ongoing pressure and risks, with 932 million shares outstanding as of their Q1’FY26 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $30.88 billion, for an intrinsic value of $33.14 per share, which is significantly above the current $19.34 despite using relatively conservative estimates, while more realistic ones get us in the $40s.

Regardless of how careful we are with the valuation assumptions, the current level is way too conservative, and the upside from here remains very hard to ignore. For the sake of the example, assuming a massive 15% discount rate and no step-up in 2028 (just a 4.5% increase instead), we get to an intrinsic value of $23.67, still above current levels.

Conclusions

HPQ remains a Strong Buy, backed by a very attractive valuation, strong cash flow and long-term macro tailwinds despite near- and medium-term pressure, all while rewarding a strong yield to wait.

There are risks, especially given all the ongoing macro uncertainty, potential for more weakness and longer-than-expected hardware issues and CEO transition risks, but the upside for long-term investors when the current problems are over remains very significant, in my opinion.

data.title: Teradyne's Rerating Is Real, But The Next Leg Depends On AI Test Intensity
data.description:
I wrote about Teradyne (TER) before. At the time, I pointed to the AI compute and networking as segments driving revenue growth, while also functioning as margin expanders. At the time, my chart analysis pointed towards a breakout on the back of this underlying trend. That setup worked very well, with the stock re-rating massively and never looking back.

Several months later, the stock still seems to be discounting the company's earning power trajectory. The company went from a cycle business to AI infra leverage in a hurry. This is the market realizing the company is no longer a mobile seasonality story, and it is becoming a "test and automation" layer in the AI infra buildout.

Where Is The Revenue Coming From?

A look at the revenue shows us that AI compute is becoming the center of the business. In the past, the company was highly dependent on mobile phones, and the underlying cycle. That was how the market saw the company in that long sideways range. The market was still seeing the observable baggage: cyclical ATE and lumpiness in the customer spending. And rightly so, test markets are concentrated and there is a limited number of clients driving purchases. That is exactly the kind of setup where Wyckoff's "composite operator" can accumulate without the stock going anywhere. Lots of supply to absorb with no consensus on a new narrative.

However, that is changing. The company is now a bet on AI CapEx, thanks to the growth in compute product revenue going from $109M in 2023 to $753 in 2025. That's a tectonic shift in the business model and led the change in perception around the stock.

Teradyne handed the market further proof. With Sales around $1.08 billion, operating profit 29% higher, and the company disclosing "over 60% AI-driven revenue" the skepticism just disappeared.

The Strategy That Makes The Model Work

The latest releases show that AI has become the main growth driver for the company. In this segment the UltraFLEXplus (FLEX test platform) appears as one of the main product lines. This moves the needle because compute testers are high ASP, with ramp concentrated in bursts, which ultimately leads to big quarterly delta in revenue. On the same token, as compute devices need more complex test patterns, test intensity rises and that leads automatically to more revenue.

Another interesting segment is the Magnum (memory test), with Magnum 7 designed for parallel memory testing, namely HBM. As memory has become a major bottleneck in the AI compute arena, and memory needs intensify, Teradyne has a major opportunity to derive more revenue from HBM and DRAM as final test content per bit is trending higher.

We can see that what moves the margin profile will ultimately be services attach. Things like extended warranties, training, and application support usually have higher gross margins, smoothing out the lumpy hardware shipment cycles. But services won't the only thing moving the needle on operating leverage. The mix shift toward high-end computing and memory configurations is likely the justification why the gross margin dipped somewhat from 59.4% to 57.2% while the operating margin expanded.

Valuation

If you look at the sales multiple for our comparative company selection, you will notice that the market is already pricing Teradyne as a premium AI infra semicap. The company trading near ASML's P/S is a bit revealing, given that the European company is usually regarded as a structurally advantaged gate keeper. For Teradyne to benefit from such a re-rate must mean that the market sees AI as a big driver for testing, and that wave will be big. Also, the company's position in compute, HBM and DRAM testing is strong. Finally, it is credible that the market is extrapolating that the company can achieve a higher steady state margin profile.

On the P/FCF side, the valuation seems more rich. But we have good reasons to believe that the market is just shrugging a depressed near term FCF and betting on a big jump in the following quarters. The justification is simple, in the business segment it is usual that EPS looks better than FCF during a ramp. The company needs build working capital (inventory), CapEx also steps up, and finally the generic trend of is always towards being consuming cash now, while generating later.

Risks

This thesis does not exist without a set of risks that are worth mapping. The AI-driven TAM expansion implies a surge that might lead to a digestion, and the market does not seem be pricing that. Even the company is telling us in the filings that the revenue is lumpy and we should not annualize a simply hot quarter. The market is pricing Teradyne as a premium AI infra play, but a digestion phase is likely to unleash a multiple compression period.

Additionally, the story also involves gaining share in compute and memory testing. We need to bear in mind that share in compute is not something owned, it is something to be contested in a highly concentrated customer base (top 5 customer represented 44% of revenue in 2025). The company can progress in absolute terms due to TAM expansion but numbers still look bad if the overall share remains low.

Margins might prove harder to improve than we currently consider due to difficulty in improving the premium mix and scaling OpEx efficiently. Margins stalling will likely mean that the EPS growth power story is done and the multiple will compress.

Finally, there are other fat tail risks that can break the multiple regardless of the management's ability to execute. Geopolitics comes to mind, but it is not the only exogenous risk.

Wrapping Up

Bringing this home, the key aspect here is that Teradyne is no longer being driven by a good smartphone year. The company is being perceived as a test and automation play in the AI buildout, and that gives you a very different earnings power profile.

The valuation is the reality check. Based on the consensus table, and peer valuation, we can see that the market is already putting a premium on the business. A look at the consensus table shows that the company is valued at 11.4x FY26 and 9.7x FY27, and that means a big chunk of the rate already happened.

The question becomes can Teradyne keep the premium multiple as the AI story remains the center of gravity? To answer this we need to consider the overall landscape for this type of company. In semicap land we generally have two buckets: one for structural gatekeepers that will hold 10x to 12x revenue multiples, and a second one for cyclical tools that will live closer to 6x and 8x revenues. This is a rough simplification, but in broad accordance to our previous peer valuation snapshot.

Therefore, considering 157M shares, the back of the envelope calculations become:

  1. bull case at 12x FY27 (12x$4.95B equals $59.4B in equity value or $378 price target);
  2. ii) the bear case at 8x FY27 (8x$4.95B equals $39.6 in equity value or $252 price target);

The risk return profile is almost symmetrical, and that basically tells us that there is room to run, but that we are no longer early to the re-rate. I rate it a buy, but I would not chase it, I would rather accumulate on dips (like the recent one) and be patient.

data.title: Devon Energy: Still Deeply Undervalued As The Coterra Merger Adds Major Upside
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Devon Energy (DVN), I called them “Still The Best Opportunity In U.S. Oil That I'm Buying,” highlighting their significant undervaluation, efficiency improvements and proactive management through a volatile environment.

Despite the stock returning about 25% since November, DVN remains a Strong Buy, backed by an attractive valuation, high-quality assets and continued operational improvements, with a solid balance sheet, strong shareholder returns and even a major merger announced with Coterra Energy (CTRA) that adds even more potential.

Internal Developments

Devon reported a solid Q4 and 2025 overall, with a slight miss on the market’s non-GAAP EPS expectations but a strong beat in terms of revenue, while the free cash flow grew very well to ~$3.12 billion, well above 2024’s $2.96 billion thanks to their acquisitions, while also reducing the number of shares significantly.

After adjusting for working capital changes, we’re looking at about $2.97 billion in 2025 and $3.17 billion in 2024, which is still very solid for a ~$27 billion market cap (after the recent increase) given the macro pressure and falling commodity prices.

DVN also advanced on their optimizations - which I’d call an advantage of this company - with an 8% reduction in LOE and GP&T per boe, with efficient capital spending despite increasing production, boasting a >15% capital efficiency improvement, with superior well productivity and greater efficiency compared to the peer average, as well as being on track to achieve their $1 billion business optimization target (85% complete as of Q4'25).

Financially, based on DVN’s latest report, we continue to see a very solid position, with current assets around their current liabilities and a very manageable amount of debt that’s well-covered by their property and equipment, supporting their flexibility and allowing them to focus on their projects instead of having to worry about some significant debt wall or refinancing, which is very important in the current environment.

Coterra Energy Merger

Devon also announced their merger with Coterra that’s expected to generate $1 billion in synergies and cost savings, building a major operator that Barclays called “too cheap and too large to ignore” during their recent Overweight rating upgrade, while the balance sheet is expected to remain very solid, with a 0.9x adjusted leverage. Note that this merger is 54% Devon, 46% Coterra, and each Coterra shareholder will get 0.7 DVN shares per each CTRA share as a result.

I covered CTRA back in November and highlighted their flexible, high-quality, multi-basin exposure with a solid inventory, plenty of natural gas production, strong cash flow and a good balance sheet, and I believe they complement Devon quite well, and the potential synergies are certainly not bad. The company’s CEO even hinted at these advantages during their Q4 Earnings Call:

The merger unites complementary portfolios with substantial and overlapping positions across the best U.S. shale basins. At the heart of this combined portfolio is a world-class position in the Delaware Basin, which will generate more than half of our total production and cash flow, backed by a decade-plus of top-tier inventory. Beyond the Delaware, the geographic diversity and balanced commodity mix provides strength throughout the volatility of the commodity price cycle. (...) In total, we expect to deliver $1 billion in annual pretax run rate synergies by year-end '27. (...) And importantly, if there are any net reduction in activity levels, these capital savings will be incremental to our announced $1 billion target. I want to emphasize that we have a strong record of delivering on these business optimization wins.

Regarding the latter, Devon announced some enhanced shareholder returns, with a new buyback authorization for more than $5 billion (subject to the Board’s approval), for a total yield of roughly 10% based on the current combined market cap, although the duration of the program remains to be seen. Note that Devon’s buybacks were significant even before this, repurchasing roughly 5% of their shares outstanding ($1.05 billion spent) over the past year, and they also plan to hike the dividend to $0.315 quarterly, for a ~31.25% increase compared to their current one.

Macro Developments

Starting this with what’s likely going to be the biggest near-term macro factor, we have the attack against Iran, followed by Iran’s threat regarding the Strait of Hormuz, which is key for international trade when it comes to oil, gas, fertilizers, and more.

Trump said that the US Navy could escort ships through the strait, but that has so far not been enough for giants like Maersk (AMKBY), with shipping giants not only avoiding the strait itself, but the entire region instead (including the Suez Canal) and instead preferring to take the longer route surrounding Africa, which can have implication on global trade well beyond these commodities.

Regardless, this remains very important on a global scale, as Iran’s retaliation can have significant consequences on countries that are key for the Petrodollar’s dominance and also natural gas/LNG giants like Qatar, which produces ~20% of global LNG, with the attack against the latter leading to an initial ~50% jump in European gas and higher pump prices.

Something very important that I want to include is the fact that if the goal here is a lasting regime change (which seems to be the case given Trump’s recent statements), this is very likely to last for much, much more than Trump’s expected few weeks.

Smaller and arguably less prepared countries like Afghanistan and Vietnam resisted for 2 decades despite having American people on the ground, and the long-term replacement still failed. Iran is a massive natural fortress, with mountains, four-season weather, a military that’s likely been preparing for something like this for decades and, very importantly, lacks an organized/armed resistance/opposition, which mark monumental differences from a military point of view.

In fact, this makes me think the goal here might be a short-term skirmish and a push for negotiations afterwards, as the end if that’s the goal is not really in sight. Regardless, every drop of uncertainty combined with high fuel/energy prices adds even more pressure to an already fragile global economy, and I wouldn’t rule out the possibility of significant slowdowns - or worse, especially if Iran’s retaliation on its neighbors continues.

Meanwhile, I wouldn’t ignore the potential to see OPEC+ opt for bigger production hikes than the ones currently planned for April, potentially offering a ceiling to how far prices can go, while the fundamentals behind oil remain relatively weak, with Wood Mackenzie recently highlighting that fundamental pressures from the surplus and weak demand (both of which would be affected by weak demand) can take over in case the blockade doesn’t last:

In this conflict, the stakes are higher still with 15 million b/d of Gulf crude and product exports under threat. Failure to quickly re-establish flows through the Strait of Hormuz could again drive prices well over US$100/bbl from Friday’s close under US$73/bbl (Brent).

Meanwhile, regarding Venezuela, I consider Exxon’s (XOM) “uninvestable” as being a perfect word to describe the current situation, as even though we’re talking about the largest reserves on the planet, we’re still looking at an oil industry marked by significant macro risks, high need for processing (being among the heaviest on the planet) and ancient infrastructure that requires massive investments, with no security guarantees for the long-term.

However, we saw Exxon recently saying they’re going to send a team to Venezuela, potentially being a first step towards re-entering the country after they left in 2007, as taking the risk suddenly makes more sense when oil prices can remain very elevated (or at least having the optionality).

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, Devon will always depend on commodity prices, and volatility is the only thing that’s guaranteed, with potential for significant swings stemming from politics, wars, speculation, weather, etc., which should always warrant a generous margin of safety, in my opinion.

Meanwhile, given the ongoing merger, there’s certainly potential for more volatility, depending on its outcome and potential to see the market disagree with moves (e.g. asset sales or refusing to do it), especially if it comes during a weak time for oil and gas.

Valuation

For this valuation DCF to Equity, assuming the same oil and gas prices as in 2025 for the entire period since it seems like a solid average, I’ll go for a combined FCF of $3 billion from Devon and $2 billion from Coterra, meaning $5 billion in FCF in 2026, adjusted for working capital changes. Note that I won’t adjust for net borrowings, as I consider the current levels of debt to be appropriate for the long-term, with leverage that remains around the general target in the industry.

Given their stated synergies and potential for continued strong execution, I’ll assume they can grow it equally to $5.75 billion in 2028 (below the $1 billion target since it’s possible that not everything will be visible in the cash flow), followed by a 5% CAGR until 2030 given the potential for more improvements, cost savings and buybacks helping the per share result, a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a very high discount rate of 12% despite their quality and strong financials, to be extra careful and take into account merger risks combined with the potential volatility.

As for the number of shares, given Devon’s 622 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report and the 762 million CTRA shares that will turn at a rate of 0.7-for-1 into DVN, I’ll assume 1.155 billion in shares outstanding.

This way, we get to an equity value of $60.71 billion and an intrinsic value of $52.57 per share, which is above the current $44.52 despite using relatively conservative base assumptions (commodity prices) and a generous discount rate. Given the spread they’ll get (54% Devon, 46% Coterra), that also means about $36.80 for CTRA compared to their current $31.15.

Meanwhile, a 10% discount rate for example - which is more appropriate for a company of this size and quality - would get us to $67.10 instead, with potential for much better numbers from many points of view.

Conclusions

Devon remains a Strong Buy for me, backed by an attractive valuation and very solid potential to continue delivering good results in the long-term, with strong financials and great shareholder returns.

There are risks to remember especially given the industry and the merger, but the overall reward greatly outweighs it, in my opinion, with significant undervaluation if we use estimates that are closer aligned to their peers.

data.title: The Trade Desk: The OpenAI Deal Looks More Than Just Rumors
data.description:
My previous bullish thesis about The Trade Desk, Inc. (TTD) did not age well at all, as the share price has halved from $48 to $24, and yesterday's 18% spike after OpenAI-related optimism has recovered the share price just partially. However, I am not ready to throw TTD into the same bucket as SaaSpocalypse stocks because the company still demonstrates robust profitability and premium growth across key metrics. Moreover, the fresh OpenAI-related report that woke up the stock up again looks like a highly probable big bullish catalyst. All in all, TTD remains a fundamentally strong and attractively valued stock, which justifies reiteration of a Strong Buy rating.

Recent developments

Since my previous coverage in early November, TTD has released Q3 and Q4 earnings, both marked with dual beats against consensus estimates. The latest quarterly earnings were solid, with a 14.3% YoY revenue growth and the operating margin expanding from 26.4% to 30.3% on a YoY basis.

As a result, the company's FCF generating capacity also improved on a YoY basis. TTD's free cash flow increased from $201 million to $259 million, increasing by almost 29% on a YoY basis. It implies that the FCF margin improved from 27% to 30.5% in Q4 2025 compared to the same quarter last year. With such a rapidly improving FCF capacity, it is no wonder that TTD's balance sheet remains a fortress with an $866 million net cash position. It makes the company well equipped to maintain its quite aggressive R&D-to-revenue ratio of high-teens.

Now, let's discuss why OpenAI-related rumors are likely to be a real big deal rather than just noise. The generative LLM giant has reportedly held early talks to partner TTD to help it scale its digital advertising business. Investors started aggressively buying TTD on these rumors and the stock delivered an 18% one-day share price increase. Below are my thoughts why TTD really has potential to partner with OpenAI (OPENAI) to help drive the digital advertising business.

First and foremost, OpenAI is a generative AI company, the area where it is pioneering and excelling. The competition among LLMs is intensifying and OpenAI doesn't have much time to invest in experiments while building the digital advertising business, especially given the fact that one of the major rival LLMs is Google's Gemini. Google is already the global digital advertising leader that also owns a very popular LLM, which puts it in a pole position here. This is the biggest reason why OpenAI highly likely needs an experienced partner in the digital advertising area.

Since we have figured out that OpenAI indeed highly likely needs a partner with expertise, lets discuss the reasons why TTD has solid chances to become such partner. When we speak about digital advertising business, the two names on the surface are Google and Meta. I think that OpenAI partnering with these two giants is extremely unlikely because they compete in the generative AI field.

Besides Google and Meta, there are two emerging AI-powered players that help businesses to drive efficiency of marketing dollars. These companies are AppLovin (APP) and Zeta Global (ZETA), but I think that they are also not perfect for OpenAI because their offerings are aimed at clients who want to optimize and squeeze more performance out of existing digital advertising engines. On the contrary, TTD is a more natural choice for businesses that are just setting up their digital advertising engines because of its pure demand-side platform for big brands and agencies. TTD is a top-tier partner for global media agencies and large brand advertisers, something that OpenAI is likely to seek to ensure a powerful start of its digital advertising business.

Moreover, large clients usually integrate TTD deeply into their workflows because of data connections, custom bidding logic, and reporting, which increases potential switching costs for all new customers. Considering this moat, I do not think that OpenAI will use TTD just like a temporary launchpad but will highly likely become a long-standing customer.

Finally, I believe that the notable portion of TTD's share price weakness so far in 2026 is explained by fears due to SaaSpocalypse. While TTD is a software stock, I think that SaaS-related fears might be overblown in this case. I think so because TTD is not a classic seat-based business as the company generates most of its revenues as a take rate on ad spending of its customers. Therefore, the seated-based exposure is very low, which makes TTD a wrong SaaSpocalypse victim.

Valuation update

TTD is down by 55% over the last 12 months and is down by 22% year-to-date, which brought its market cap to approximately $12 billion. Meanwhile, we saw that TTD's financial performance remains strong and other fundamental criteria are also impressive. Therefore, TTD's multiples are currently quite attractive. Of course, some of the ratios are still higher than the sector median. However, it is vital to keep in mind that TTD is expected to maintain double-digit EPS growth over the next several years while the sector median EPS growth is below 10%. Therefore, it is vital to look at the PEG ratio, which suggests massive undervaluation compared to the sector for TTD.

The above comparative table suggests that the 1-year forward adjusted sector median P/E ratio is 13.89. Considering TTD's superior growth compared to the sector and its solid profitability, I think that the stock should trade at a moderate discount compared to the sector. Determining the premium is always a tricky exercise because it requires a lot of personal judgment. To estimate the approximate ballpark, I would like to compare TTD's profitability to the sector median. According to the comparison below, various profitability ratios of TTD are higher than the sector by several times. The narrowest 25% difference appears to be in terms of EBITDA margin, which is an important ratio as it reflects operational profitability.

Therefore, let's assume that TTD should trade at a 25% compared to the sector median. To calculate the multiple for my analysis, I therefore need to inflate the aforementioned sector's P/E ratio of 13.89 by 25%. It yields a multiple of 17.4, which in turn should be multiplied by the consensus EPS estimate of $2.38 for FY2027. The product of this multiplication is $41, which is my 12-month target price for TTD.

Risks update

As I mentioned in the introduction, my previous bullish thesis on TTD did not age well at all. I had high conviction in early November as well, but the stock still nosedived by almost 40% since then. I believe that large part of this share price weakness is explained by panic selloffs due to the significantly deteriorated market sentiment over the last four months. Since the world currently witnesses rapidly evolving geopolitical landscape, investors might remain on sidelines for a while. Moreover, it is important to say that investing in a stock that currently trades four times cheaper than its ATH is an inherently bold and long-term bet that requires a lot of patience.

Moreover, investing in TTD in early March does not look like a great idea from the share price seasonality perspective. Over the last decade, March has been historically the second weakest month for TTD with just a 40% win-rate. Therefore, we might see another period of share price weakness after yesterday's spike due to seasonal reasons.

Bottom line

To conclude, I believe that TTD remains a Strong Buy despite the fact that my previous thesis did not age well at all. The company is expected to continue delivering double-digit EPS growth over the next several years, which will highly likely significantly outpace the sector's pace. I am willing to double down at current levels, but readers should keep in mind that I am a long-term investor.

data.title: Sticking With Baxter At The Lows
data.description:
At a Citi conference last week, Baxter International (BAX) chief financial officer Joel Grade made an interesting admission. On his company's Q3 conference call in late October, chief executive officer Andrew Hider had said he anticipated that the company would hold an Investor Day this year. Such an event would make sense, given that Hider took over only in August, and has been tasked with a wide-ranging and potentially complex effort to turn the business around.

On the Q4 call, however, Hider said that Baxter had "decided to hold off". Last week, Grade was asked why. The CFO noted that workload was an issue: the hours spent poring over Baxter's business were hours that would be lost in preparation for an Investor Day. A new, flattened, organizational chart is playing a role as well. But the final reason was the most telling:

And then third, just being candid, I think getting a few quarters under our belt where we continue to perform with consistency, I think puts us in a better position to have a really credible Investor Day that says, 'hey, here's a look forward' and then with confidence, people can [trust that] -- I think that's just some humility that I think is the reality is where we're at today.

In essence, Grade is saying that holding an Investor Day in 2026 is not particularly useful, because the market wouldn't believe much of what Baxter executives had to say. In this, he's almost certainly not wrong.

Hider's tenure has begun with a pair of disappointing releases, including sharply below-consensus guidance for this year. Management sentiment toward two key risks already has proven to be overly optimistic; any broader positivity at this point probably would be dismissed.

It's not hard to see the trading in Baxter stock — which hit a 20-year low late last year, and has threatened those levels twice in recent weeks — as proving Grade's point. Investors simply do not trust Baxter right now.

Of course, as always in these situations, that lack of trust can either be a sign of danger, or of opportunity. For now, after arguing for the stock at similar levels last year, I'm sticking with the latter approach. It's not at all difficult, however, to see why the market feels differently.

No, BAX Isn't Cheap

In highlighting the risks in BAX last year, I noted that the trajectory at the time suggested the company was at real risk for a sub-$2 adjusted earnings per share print in 2026. That's likely to play out, with the outlook initially set at $1.85-$2.05 against a $2.25 consensus. (I'm usually more positive than the sell side than most, but in this case I'm completely confused as to why estimates remained as high as they did.)

The problem then, and now, is that at that earnings level, BAX stock really isn't cheap by any reasonable fundamental analysis. A sub-10x multiple to the midpoint of this year's earnings outlook sounds inexpensive on its face, but an overlevered balance sheet (near 4x EBITDA based on guidance) is a big contributor there.

Given the trajectory of revenue of late (and gross profit dollars falling), those multiples aren't terrible, but they don't make BAX some kind of deep value play. Valuation here isn't a sign that the market is missing something; it's instead a reflection of the fact that investors fear that profits (and margins) will continue on a flat-to-down trajectory. That's not an unreasonable fear at all.

Run Away, Run Away!

Given that, there is a case for taking losses in Baxter stock here. This kind of profile — an over levered, old-line business that does not look nearly as strong as it once did — has destroyed ample investor capital over the past 20 years. Amplifying that broader issue is the sector-specific struggles healthcare has had of late.

And particularly after Q4, there is the company-focused risk that investors here are betting on a turnaround under a CEO whose tenure got off to a pretty rocky start. Beyond the soft guidance, the two big pieces of news from Q4 centered on risks to the business on which investors have focused for a while. In both cases, Baxter and Hider overpromised and underdelivered.

One big concern here has been around the trend of so-called "fluid conservation". A Baxter facility in North Carolina produced about 60% of the nation's supply of intravenous fluid bags. When the facility was flooded during Hurricane Helene, hospitals responded by limiting IV use in favor of techniques such as 'IV push' (ie, syringes instead of bags). Baxter insisted that as production normalized, hospitals would return to the old way of doing things (known as 'IV drip'); as recently as the Q3 call, the company expected continuing recovery, albeit with continued, more moderate effects in 2026.

That outlook seemed to change after Q4, with Grade instead projecting "a new baseline" for the IV Solutions business. Announced workforce reductions seem to confirm that Baxter now sees a 'new normal' rather than a temporary rebound.

Similarly, the timeline for a return to market of the company's Novum pump keeps slipping. After Q2, the company was "hoping" for a return to the market in 2025; after Q3, the pause in shipments was extended into this year; now, it will last at least through 2026. Novum isn't a huge product (yet) in terms of revenue, but given the nature of hospital supplies in the category and its previously expected boost to overall growth, its delay is a problem simply in terms of the business.

And, of course, the combination of essentially whiffing on two core investor worries that existed before Hider took over is a big hit to Baxter's credibility. That's exactly that point that CFO Glade was making at the conference last week: the market does not trust Baxter, and it's not terribly difficult to see why that is.

The Contrarian Pitch For BAX Stock

As with the piece last year, this is quite a negative opening for what remains a bullish recommendation. But as was the case last year — and in most situations like this — the risks need to be delineated and understood. There are real risks to the downside here. There are real reasons why BAX stock is once again flirting with a two-decade low.

But at the same time, there are some reasons for optimism when looking closely. Valuation isn't a reason alone to own BAX here — but it's certainly not particularly aggressive. Again, the market is pricing Baxter for flat to negative profit growth — and looking beyond 2026, there are reasons to believe that is too conservative.

The first is that for a long time Baxter was an excellent business. It was the crown jewel in Third Point's portfolio, with impressive operating margins and solid growth. The Hill-Rom acquisition changed that perception (and reality), but there are still strong end markets and products here. The surgery business (~10% of sales) has been a consistent growth, and had a strong Q4. Care & Connectivity Solutions (~18% of revenue) too is driving sales and posted a strong spike in orders in Q4 2025.

There are core issues here, but in the unaffected part of the portfolio performance simply is not as bad as sentiment suggests. Nor are end markets: Baxter executives (consistent with those elsewhere in the sector) continue to say that customer demand is reasonably solid.

Meanwhile, looking to 2027 (and perhaps even the second half of this year), there are ways for Baxter to jumpstart growth and start returning margins toward past levels. From a broad standpoint, the company simply has some certainty with fluid conservation at least, which will allow for more efficient production and improved margins.

At the conference last week, Glade noted supply and production challenges which should start to be fixed in the second half of this year. A new go-to-market model should start to show benefits around the same time, simply by removing layers of bureaucracy and thus cost. The CFO was asked directly if gross and operating margins would start to expand in the second half, and he answered simply, "Yes."

There are new launches coming in the Healthcare Systems & Technologies. There still is at least the potential for a recovery in the IV space; Novum should return to shipments at some point. There are paths for Baxter to get back to consistent top-line growth, while the cost structure adapts to the new realities in certain end markets and boosts margins. There's also the fact that Hider, who has a strong track record starting out at Danaher (DHR) and then leading ATS Corporation (ATS) to market-beating performance. (It's a sign of Hider's tenure there that ATS stock dropped nearly 9% the day Baxter announced it had hired him.)

And the flip side of the risks here are the rewards. The combination of Baxter getting back to revenue growth plus margin expansion, while adding a deleveraging component, suggests the path to triple-digit upside. $1 billion in debt reduction, three-year cumulative EBITDA growth of ~25%, and a 10x multiple means BAX would roughly double.

Essentially, BAX is just a straight bet on the turnaround working. And that case has been dented, and perhaps delayed, by the disappointments in Q4 — but it's not broken. I wrote a year ago that Baxter shouldn't be written off; even with Hider's tenure off to a rocky start, that's still the case.

data.title: Samsara: No HALO, No Problem - Shares Rise After A Strong Q4
data.description:
Samsara Inc. (IOT) is a clear victim of the SaaSpocalypse. Indeed, it’s at the other end of the HALO (heavy asset, low obsolescence) trade. Down 34% since my December Buy rating coming into its Q4 earnings report, there was some hope for a turnaround. Like the iShares Expanded Tech & Software ETF (IGV), IOT noted a notable February low. Shares then jumped after the quarterly numbers crossed the wires.

Today, I reiterate a Buy rating and am sticking with my $40 intrinsic value target. I’ll provide refreshed fundamental numbers, along with an updated look at the technicals, after what has been a wild start to 2026.

Samsara Down Sharply YoY Amid the HALO Trade

In March, Samsara reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.18 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.13, while revenue of $444 million (up more than 28% from the same period last year) was a material $22 million beat. AI-fueled growth last year and a sanguine company forecast sent the stock higher, and the firm now expects 21-22% revenue growth for FY 2027, along with accelerating annual recurring revenue (ARR) and AI expansion.

Shares were 12% higher in the premarket the following day. That’s actually not a wild move, considering that the options market had priced in a 12.1% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring this week. If IOT sticks the gain by the close, that would be three consecutive very strong post-earnings pops. IOT, now a nearly $18 billion market cap company, does not pay a dividend and traded with a very high 88% implied volatility coming into the report. The software firm has a high short interest of 9%, potentially contributing to the jump as of this writing.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the standout backward-looking figure was a 33% YoY rise in ARR to $1.9 billion. The growth was driven by strong momentum among its large customers, with ARR from $100k customers growing by 37% to $1.2 billion. The company also improved its efficiency, reporting a record non-GAAP operating margin of 21% for the quarter and reaching GAAP profitability with a GAAP operating margin of 2%.

Samsara: ARR & Revenue Milestones

Operationally, Samsara continues to scale its "Connected Operations Cloud," capturing over 25 trillion data points annually and expanding its reach across diverse industries, including food service, waste management, and chemical carriers. Looking forward to FY 27, the firm provided a generally constructive outlook, seeing total revenue between $1.965-$1.975 billion, which would be roughly 21%-22% growth.

The 30% ARR growth takes down the bear case, in my view. That percentage was far above Street estimates, which included some negative takes, including hints at growth plateaus and falling momentum. Samsara boasts solid top-line trends, along with a lean back-office, which should help to keep costs in check.

On the call, CEO Sanjit Biswas introduced a roadmap for specialized AI agents designed to act as "force multipliers" for back-office teams. It’s a sign that IOT is not just a telematics/GPS company.

FY 2027 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be very strong in FY 2027 (which is now underway). Operating per-share earnings may rise to $0.68 this year and potentially top $1 toward the end of FY 2029.

Along a steady near-20% revenue growth path, there have been a high 11 sell-side EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just a trio of downgrades. Finally, profitability trends appear to be improving, with free cash flow per share of $0.34 coming into the Q4 report.

Samsara: Revenue & Earnings Forecasts, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, I am sticking with the numbers I outlined late last year—but am still very impressed with the Q4 top and bottom lines. Using a 55x price-to-earnings ratio on FY 2027 numbers, we arrive at a fair value of $37.40.

Of course, there is a period shift, so that adds a slight amount to the intrinsic value estimate. Keep in mind that the current PEG ratio is very low at 1.15x.

Samsara: Premium Valuation Multiples

Key risks for Samsara include the rising costs of memory and storage, which could eat into profit growth in the coming quarters. That will make its supply chain and sourcing very key in the short run.

What’s more, geopolitical risks are a clear and present concern, but there didn’t appear to be any major hiccups in the previous quarter, nor on the call. Of course, competition remains high, and that could limit customer growth in the year to come.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2027 earnings date of Thursday, June 4, AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares still well below my fundamental value estimate, IOT’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the stock has hurdled the key $31-$32 range with the post-earnings rise. It’s critical to hold that level into the week’s close. The climb also brings shares above the falling short-term 50-day moving average, while the 200dma is up near $37. The onus is still on the bulls here, given the high amount of volume by price up to the low $40s.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It tagged a multi-year extreme in oversold conditions last month, and the V-bottom is quite encouraging today. I expect the RSI to rise to 70 in the coming days. But a downtrend resistance line off the early 2025 peak may come into play close to $44.

Overall, a bullish false breakdown could be in the works here, and given the high short interest, further upside is definitely doable.

IOT: Back Above $32 in the Premarket, Falling 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a Buy rating on IOT. The stock plunged immediately after my December analysis, but shares have come storming back. I see upside both fundamentally and potentially on the charts after a strong set of Q4 results.

data.title: Early Impact Of Iran War Is Low, But Economic Risks Are Rising
data.description:
The economic fallout from the war in Iran has been limited for the US so far. But as the conflict continues, the effects will become increasingly clear.

The main risks: slower economic growth and higher inflation, driven largely by higher energy prices. It’s too early to confidently estimate how the war will influence those factors, but as the war – one week old tomorrow – persists, the macro price tag will surely rise.

Because economic data is reported with a lag, the blowback will take time to show up in official releases. For next month’s first-quarter GDP release, for instance, even if the war continues through the end of March, the effects may be hard to spot.

The Atlanta Fed’s Mar. 2 nowcast of Q1 GDP: +3.0%, marking a solid rebound from the sluggish 1.4% increase in Q4. Revisions are likely before the official April 30 release, but the war-related impact may be modest, given that the attack began late in the quarter.

February data published earlier in the week underscores ongoing strength in private‑sector hiring and services activity. ADP on Wednesday said that companies added 63,000 jobs last month, the strongest monthly gain since July. Meanwhile, the ISM Services Index rose to its highest growth reading in February in nearly four years.

The outlook for Q2 is more vulnerable. US and Israeli officials have said the war could continue for several weeks, which would give the inflationary and slower-growth effects more oxygen. The degree and extent of pain these effects could bring is unclear for now, but markets are already reflecting greater caution relative to the pre-war outlook.

One of the clearest signs of shifting sentiment relates to monetary policy. The rate cuts that were priced in via Fed funds futures for June are now seen as unlikely. September is currently the earliest month in which odds favor an initial cut.

Earlier this week, Cleveland Fed President Beth Hammack called for an extended pause on rate cuts. Part of the calculus is that economic activity appears to be firming in Q1. Add in the inflation-related uncertainty from the war, and she sees a stronger case for holding rates steady. “I want to see evidence that we are making progress on the inflation side of our mandate to have more confidence in my forecast,” she said.

Treasury yields have remained relatively steady since the war started, trading within the range that’s prevailed in recent months. But the market is starting to pick up on the potential for higher inflation. The policy-sensitive 2-year yield has increased every day this week, rising to 3.59% on Thursday.

Higher yields are likely until there are signs that the war, if not ending, is winding down. For now, the opposite seems to be the likely path ahead for the immediate future.

The FT this morning is reporting: Qatar, the world’s second-largest producer of liquified natural gas, says the war will force Persian Gulf energy exports to end “within days.”

“This will bring down the economies of the world,” predicts Saad al-Kaabi, Qatar’s energy minister. “If this war continues for a few weeks, GDP growth around the world will be impacted. Everybody’s energy price is going to go higher. There will be shortages of some products, and there will be a chain reaction of factories that cannot supply.”

Hyperbole? Maybe, but making the case that the fallout will be limited is becoming tougher every day the war continues and energy infrastructure in the Gulf comes under additional strain.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: EUAD: Europe Has No Choice, And This ETF Could Be The Big Beneficiary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying the Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense (BATS:EUAD) ETF. This is my first article about companies in the European aerospace and defense sector; however, I have two investment theses in State Street SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense (NYSEARCA:XAR), an American defense ETF. In this article, my objective is to answer a very pertinent question from a follower. Let's take a closer look at ETFs. The dynamics behind the thesis, the perspectives, and the valuation.

Context

Recently a follower asked me a very pertinent question. He analyzed my articles about XAR, a defense ETF, and asked me if there were other options for investing in this topic, as he believed that the companies in that ETF were already well priced.

In this article I will answer this follower's question, and without further ado, there is a defense ETF that has even greater potential than XAR. The dilemma is that the investor will have to take more risk if they choose it.

Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF

The asset in question is the Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF, and as you can deduce, this is a European defense and security ETF. But before going into more detail about this asset, it is interesting to note that we also have the WDEF pair, which even has a more attractive expense ratio. However, due to its robustness, longer history, and attractive expense ratio, my option in this segment of European defense companies is EUAD.

Well, as we can see above, EUAD has $1.47 billion in assets under management and an expense ratio of 0.50%. This asset is quite bold; its composition has only 14 holdings, and the three main holdings represent 53.1% of the total.

The EUAD is designed to track the performance of the STOXX Europe Total Market Aerospace & Defense Index. This index tracks European stocks related to the aerospace and defense segments.

Why a European Defense ETF?

I imagine that this is the investor's biggest question at the moment, and basically there are several reasons.

The United States has the greatest military potential in the world, and we don't even need data on defense spending compared to GDP or metrics like that, just analyze the following: Russia is four years away and is unable to achieve its objectives in Ukraine, China hasn't even started an attempted invasion of Taiwan, meanwhile, the Trump administration captured the former Venezuelan leader Maduro in enemy territory without any casualties, and eliminated Ayatolah Khamenei on the first day of confrontation in Iran. The discrepancy is gigantic.

Fundamentally, this implies that American military relations with its suppliers (defense companies) are very mature, so the asymmetry is smaller. Therefore, timing is more important here, and perhaps that's why my recommendation on XAR was quite assertive.

But what about Europe? European countries have no choice but to invest in their defense. The relationship between Europeans and Americans has drastically shaken with the Trump administration, and unlike the United States, which is geographically "protected" by an ocean on each coast, Europe has close conflicts in several regions.

Europe has been dealing for some years with the Russian threat to countries close to Ukraine and also to the Nordic countries. A few months ago, an uncomfortable situation with American allies occurred in Greenland. Finally, the developments in the conflict in Iran generated attacks on Cyprus and even Turkey, which is a member of NATO. Therefore, European countries almost have an obligation to increase their investments in defense.

European Leaders Announcements

Given all the events described, Europe is in the biggest defense investment cycle since the Cold War. In 2024, Europe reached the tenth consecutive year of increasing military spending growth. Spending grew from 1.6% of GDP in 2023 to 1.9% of GDP in 2024. In 2025, it is estimated that spending will exceed 2% of GDP.

Several countries, such as Germany and Poland, show growth in military spending not seen since the Second World War, and the conflict situation in Iran even caused the president of France to sing the anthem in front of a nuclear submarine.

Some investors believe that Europe has little capacity to increase military spending as economic growth is expected to be low there, and I disagree. Europe has lower interest rates than other developed economies, and this could soon boost GDP.

Additionally, as we can see below, Germany (the bloc's economic engine) has plenty of room to increase its debt/GDP ratio compared to its peers, and it is interesting to remember that the country played a central role in the Second World War.

Therefore, the increase in demand for products and services from European defense companies is certain, and I would go even further. I would say that this sector is the main candidate to reduce the premium of American companies compared to European companies.

Valuation

As EUAD holdings are well known and have mature operations, I will analyze this ETF based on the price/earnings multiple and compare it with XAR, an American defense ETF that I have a recommendation to buy.

As we can see above, EUAD trades at 30x earnings, and although its multiple has been rising over the last 12 months, it is significantly below XAR's 46x earnings multiple.

As ETFs have several companies in their composition, each with its own peculiarities, I will define a multiple exit target based on the comparison between the two ETFs.

I do not intend to claim that EUAD will trade at the same multiple as XAR, as this would take years; however, it is interesting to note that EUAD at its highest multiple has never reached the minimum multiple of XAR.

Therefore, if my thesis comes to fruition, EUAD could surpass its highest historical multiple and possibly trade at 36x earnings (XAR's minimum multiple over the last 12 months). In this scenario, there is an upside of 20% in this ETF, and based on this analysis, I recommend buying EUAD, and I am quite confident with my recommendation, as I see that Seeking Alpha's Quant tools also recommend buying this ETF.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

There are several risks to my bullish thesis. The first is that Europe is made up of a block of countries, so decisions are not centralized and tend to occur more slowly. Additionally, several leaders have a history of not keeping promises.

Another important fact is that, in my opinion, European inaction for several years in this segment created some additional difficulties. This generated serious strategic problems, such as the high dependence on exports for European companies in this ETF.

A great example was the problem caused by dependence on titanium imports from the Russian company VSMPO-Avisma in the midst of the Ukrainian War. This event highlighted the challenges in the supply chains that these companies can suffer in a war environment. It is no surprise that EUAD's NAV fell 1.33% in one month, right at the time of the outbreak of the war in Iran.

The point is that when conflicts of this type occur, investors run to safe havens, like the dollar, and like the American defense companies themselves, which have managed to mitigate several risks in their supply chains over time.

In this sense, it is possible that it will take years for European leaders and executives of European companies in this ETF to adjust various strategic points of their operations regarding the supply chain. Therefore, I would say that this ETF should benefit in a hostile environment, where countries are concerned about increasing their security; however, as situations escalate into real wars, American defense ETFs are even more targeted.

The Bottom Line

My objective with this article was to answer a follower's question, which may actually be the question of many other investors. Are there still any assets related to the aerospace and defense industry with a good risk-return ratio? In my opinion, yes, and it is the EUAD.

European stocks generally trade at a deep discount to the United States, and geopolitical developments in recent years make greater investment in defense and security inevitable. This is a great opportunity for several European companies, especially defense companies.

Based on this analysis, I recommend buying EUAD. The premium of American stocks over European stocks is justifiable, but from time to time these dynamics change, and in my opinion, the European aerospace and defense sector could be the great candidate to reduce this gap.

data.title: February Jobs Report: Payrolls Decline As Strikes Impact Healthcare Sector
data.description:
Equity investors appear to be going through a gut check in recent weeks. A combination of AI-related fears, geopolitical conflicts, rising oil prices, and general economic uncertainty have all contributed to a somber mood across broader markets.

Perhaps adding misery to existing pain was the release of today's job release, which showed 92K job losses, well below the gains of 50K that were expected.

Heading in, broader indexes were nursing steep sell offs across multiple days, with the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI) closing nearly 800 points lower on the Thursday prior to Friday’s job release.

These losses were echoed by both the broader S&P 500 (SPY) and the tech-heavy, Nasdaq Composite (NDX). This came in conjunction with the rise in the global energy benchmark, Brent Crude (CL1:COM), to above $85/barrel.

While I believe today's job release serves as a negative catalyst, I don't believe one month of negative data settles the score for the labor market, especially considering the distortions caused by strike activity in a key employment sector. Here’s everything you need to know about the February jobs release.

What Sectors Lost Jobs In February?

Unlike the prior month, February’s employment report didn’t really provide investors with a clear pocket of strength across the labor market. In fact, most of the major sectors showed losses during the month.

Perhaps the most newsworthy development was within the health care sector, which has been one of the most reliable sources of job growth over the past several years. In February, however, employment in the sector declined by 28K jobs, reversing part of the sizable gain recorded in January.

The drop was largely attributed to strike activity, particularly within physicians’ offices, which shed roughly 37K during the month. Hospitals did add about 12K jobs, but that wasn’t nearly enough to offset the broader pullback. Over the past year, health care had averaged roughly 36K new jobs per month, making February’s decline stand out even more.

Elsewhere, most industries, including manufacturing, construction, retail, and professional services, were largely flat or negative.

Employment in information services, for example, continued its downward trend, declining by 11K jobs, while federal government employment fell by another 10K positions, continuing a broader decline that began late last year.

Unemployment and Wage Growth Trend

The job losses during the month likely contributed to the uptick in unemployment to 4.4% from the 4.3% that was expected.

While the uptick is notable, the figure continues to suggest employers appear hesitant or downright bearish on payrolls, but they are also showing a similar reluctance to part ways with existing workers.

As I’ve written previously, this dynamic has been one of the defining features of the current labor market cycle. Job growth has slowed noticeably from the pace seen in prior years, yet unemployment has continued to track within a relatively narrow range.

For now, that balance indicates to me that businesses are opting to hold onto staff while waiting for more clarity around the broader economic environment.

That said, there are signs that layoffs could begin to filter further into future data. Large corporations have already begun trimming headcount. Morgan Stanley (MS), for example, recently moved to cut roughly 3% of its workforce, or about 2,500 employees, despite reporting a strong 2025.

Similar reductions across technology and financial firms could gradually push the unemployment rate higher in the months ahead as those layoffs begin to appear in official labor market data.

Fed Policy Implications

Much like the January report before it, I don’t believe February’s jobs data will alter the near-term outlook for Fed policy, even if the data was significantly worse than expected

Market expectations reflect that stance quite clearly. According to the CME FedWatch Tool, investors are assigning virtually no probability to a rate change at the upcoming March meeting. In other words, the market is almost fully convinced that the Fed will stand pat later this month.

What has shifted somewhat in recent weeks, however, is the timing of a potential rate cut. Earlier in the year, markets were leaning toward a June or July move. Those expectations have since been pushed further out, with the first meaningful odds of a reduction now not appearing until September at the earliest.

Complicating the policy outlook further is the approaching end of Jerome Powell’s term in May. Powell has recently found himself at the center of political pressure surrounding interest rates and the Fed’s independence. His expected successor, Kevin Warsh, could inherit a central bank facing heightened scrutiny over monetary policy decisions in the months ahead.

Final Thoughts on the February Jobs Report

If investors were looking to go home Friday with an ounce of good news, they certainly didn’t receive it with the February jobs report.

The report ended up delivering an even more somber ending to what has already been a difficult stretch for equity investors. The data ultimately showed that 92K jobs were lost during the month, marking a far weaker outcome than many observers had anticipated.

Against an already fragile market sentiment, the downside surprise only adds another layer of uncertainty for investors trying to navigate an increasingly volatile environment.

The timing of the release couldn’t be more unfortunate. Over the past several weeks, markets have been contending with a wave of negative catalysts ranging from AI-driven concerns across the technology sector, escalating geopolitical tensions tied to the ongoing conflict in Iran, and rising oil prices.

Those forces have already weighed heavily on broader indexes, and the unexpected deterioration in payroll growth does little to improve the mood heading into the weekend.

That said, I don’t believe that one month’s data redefines the broader labor narrative. As discussed earlier, unemployment remains relatively contained, and hiring trends have been cooling for some time.

Looking ahead, I continue to believe the broader theme for markets remains selectivity rather than momentum. With geopolitical tensions, AI-driven disruptions, and higher energy prices still hovering over the market, maintaining a defensive posture within portfolios still appears the best move, in my view.

data.title: Nvidia: Why The Permit Fears Don't Change Bullish Case
data.description:
Nvidia Remains A "Buy" After Its Q4 And Following The News On Requiring Permits

After my bullish upgrade on Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) in mid-November, when the firm released its Q3 earnings, the stock price fell by over 7%, underperforming the broader market, which is a rarity for Nvidia since AI took over the headlines. Despite this lack of bullish momentum in the price action, the firm's financials remain solid - and it's not just about profitability. In fiscal Q4 2026, the company's total sales increased by 73% YoY to $68.13 billion, helping NVDA beat the consensus once again (by almost 3% or $1.90 billion, based on Seeking Alpha). On the bottom line, we've seen a YoY growth of over 82% (to $1.62), which was also above the consensus (by 5.32%).

The main market's concern, in my understanding, is about the longevity of hyperscaler CAPEX - we're now watching the widest "death jaw pattern," as Crescat Capital's analysts put it in their February note, when we match the mega-cap tech FCF flows with the S&P 500's (SPY, SP500) momentum:

In addition to that, the recent headlines regarding potential global U.S. export permits have put another layer of pressure on both NVDA and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) stocks.

Despite all the past and fresh fears around NVDA, I believe the stock is poised for continued strength as the management's plans play out and new offerings hit the market throughout the next few quarters.

There are a few reasons for staying positive on Nvidia's prospects.

First, the transition to the Blackwell architecture is progressing seamlessly, and it's already leading to strong financial results such as a profitability boost - in Q4, Nvidia's GAAP and adjusted GP margins expanded to 75.0% and 75.2%, respectively, and the ramp of the Blackwell architecture was cited as one of the main reasons for that margin expansion. The demand for Nvidia's chips remains so high that even older generations like Hopper and the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are still sold out in the cloud, per management's commentary.

With NVIDIA infrastructure in high demand, even Hopper and much of the 6-year-old Ampere-based products are sold out in the cloud. Nearly a year has passed since the release of our Grace Blackwell NVL72 systems. Today, nearly 9 gigawatts of infrastructure on Blackwell are deployed and consumed by the major cloud service providers, hyperscalers, AI model makers and enterprises.

Source: NVDA's Q4 earnings call.

From what I see, the firm's customers are absorbing its capacity as fast as Nvidia can produce it, and it can be seen from the Q4 data that the market decided to meet with skepticism.

Second, the risks that emerged following Bloomberg's news about export permits are likely phantom. In the article, it was revealed that the U.S. Commerce Department has drafted regulations that would restrict AI chip shipments globally without American approval. But thenm a spokesman came out with a post on X stating that the reports about them returning to the AI diffusion rule were not accurate.

The Commerce Department is committed to promoting secure exports of the American tech stack. We successfully advanced exports through our historic Middle East agreements, and there are ongoing internal government discussions about formalizing that approach. Today there was reporting that we were returning to the AI diffusion rule. We will not. It was burdensome, overreaching, and disastrous.

Source: U.S. Commerce Department's X post.

The Trump administration has repeatedly stated that it wants the world to use American AI, so it would be incorrect to assume that the new draft rules would function as an export ban on Nvidia. I agree that it's a geopolitical tool to ensure the U.S. wins in today's sovereign AI race, but that was a well-known fact before, so I think it was already priced into NVDA's stock long before Bloomberg's report came out. Also, it's important to understand that any draconian global bans on Nvidia's chips will only boost the attractiveness of foreign competitors like China's Huawei - that's not what the U.S. wants, obviously.

As long as Nvidia remains supply-constrained, not demand-constrained, any foreign entity's massive GPU order delays will simply be sold to a domestic hyperscaler or an enterprise customer, who are waiting in line.

While we expect tightness in the supply for our advanced architectures to persist, we remain confident in our ability to capitalize on the growth opportunity ahead with our scale, expansive supply chain and the long-standing partnerships continuing to serve us well.

Source: NVDA's CFO on the earnings call.

Third, Nvidia's lack of bullish reaction on what's already here - the agentic AI inflection point - doesn't make sense to me, as it should have repriced the stock to new heights on the Q4 call when the CEO introduced a new mantra: "Compute equals revenues":

The token generation rate is growing exponentially. And the same thing with, of course, OpenAI, their demand is incredible. And so the more compute that they can stand online, bring online, the faster their revenues will grow. And that goes back to the comment that I was saying that inference is revenues, that compute equals revenues now in this new world. And in a lot of ways, that's the reason why we say it's a new industrial revolution. There are new factories, new infrastructure being build and this new way of doing computing is not going to go back. And so, to the extent that we believe that producing tokens is going to be the future of computing, which I believe and I think largely the industry believes, then we're going to be building out this capacity from this point forward and continue to expand from here.

Source: NVDA's CEO on the Q4 call, emphasis added by Oakoff.

With agentic systems running continuously in the background, the demand for inference compute is skyrocketing, which is extremely bullish for cloud providers that can see the token activity translating into real dollars in real time, and it's indirectly bullish for Nvidia's racks and architectures as they're still the most power-efficient in the industry. I don't see NVDA losing this leading position any time soon. At CES 2026, they presented the Vera Rubin platform that should commence production shipments in fiscal 2H 2027 - this new architecture reduces inference token costs by up to 10x compared to Blackwell, according to management, and is likely to capture additional demand from enterprises and hyperscalers that are developing their AI agentic systems. So, Nvidia shouldn't become demand-constrained in the next few years, at least, in my understanding.

Fourth, following the negative news and after adjusting for the market's fears, NVDA has become relatively cheap when we match its valuation multiples with what's coming for its earnings/sales growth. According to Seeking Alpha's consensus data, NVDA's sales and EPS should grow at 5-year CAGRs of 11.6% and 11.2%, respectively (I took FY2027 as a base year for calculation). It might look modest compared to what is seen in the semis industry these days, but it's actually a lot for a $4.45 trillion market cap firm that a) has an undeniable moat in the hottest semis niche and b) trades at only ~17x forward earnings (fiscal FY2028):

In my November article, I used pretty bullish assumptions to figure out NVDA's fair value per share:

By the end of 2027, NVDA can keep its 40x earnings multiple in place, and if the trends in its sales and margins stay in place and NVDA keeps beating the consensus as it did before, we can assign a premium on its current EPS consensus, like I did in my previous update. With a 3-5% premium on their FY2027 EPS and with a 40x P/E ratio, my price target arrives at $277.89/share.

Today, I wouldn't go that bold. I'd take only 25-30x for FY2028 forward earnings because competition is on the rise from both semiconductor peers like AMD and in-house hyperscalers' solutions (Maia, Trainium, TPUs, etc.). Anyway, I'd take FY2028 consensus EPS with a slight premium of only 3-5% as I did last time to adjust for Nvidia's strong beating track record. All these updated inputs lead me to a 12-month target of $307.16/share:

$10.74 * 1.04 * 27.5x = $307.16/share (upside of +67%)

Source: Oakoff's calculations.

As you can see, despite the fact that I've taken a smaller exit P/E multiple for NVDA, the stock has become even more attractive in terms of its growth potential (last time I saw an undervaluation of ~46%).

With all the above bullish reasons in mind, I think Nvidia deserves to be rated a Buy after its prolonged consolidation.

Risks To Keep In Mind

As I noted last time, investors should understand that Nvidia is naturally at the epicenter of the AI boom, and it may be priced to perfection to some skeptics even if the stock trades at just 17x forward earnings. By buying NVDA stock right now, investors are effectively accepting a series of highly optimistic assumptions to justify Nvidia's $4.45 trillion valuation - if any of those assumptions fail to materialize, the forward 17x P/E can turn into 30-40x as the net income dips instead of rising higher. That would likely mean the ultimate break of the current bullish trend for NVDA.

Let's assume that NVDA's EPS grows by only 20% in FY2028 instead of 30.22% that the consensus currently prices in. In this scenario, the market is likely to assign a lower multiple, seeing a cyclical turn ahead - semis don't trade at extraordinary multiples historically because they're sensitive to cycles in general. So, if NVDA's FY2028 P/E drops to 15x, and the EPS itself comes in at ~$9.90, the resulting price target should be ~$148.5/share. It gives us a downside potential of about 19% to the current price.

Your Takeaway

The risks are high, and the narrative remains hammered by some events that I don't think can materially change my fundamentally bullish thesis on NVDA. I couldn't have thought we'd come to the point where buying NVDA is effectively a contrarian trade, but here we are.

In my opinion, the narrative should change when the market realizes that Nvidia's bullish cycle isn't over, and that the valuation has already become very tempting on a forward basis. It might be time to buy NVDA now as it's touching its 200-day moving average, and the consolidation seems to be taking too long.

In such cases, the stock should move up or down to interrupt the consolidation, and in my opinion, we have more reasons to expect a move up rather than a move down. I remain bullish on NVDA's prospects, keeping my Buy unchanged.

Good luck with your investments!
